solution: First shift the input by [[h]], then evaluate \( f \), then multiply by the stretch factor [[A]] and finally add the vertical shift [[k]]: \( g([[x0]]) = [[A]]\cdot f([[x0]]-[[h]]) + ([[k]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to apply the stretch factor [[A]] and vertical shift [[k]] together with the horizontal shift.
This forgets the horizontal shift by [[h]], evaluating \( f \) at \( [[x0]] \) directly.
This shifts left instead of right, using \( x + [[h]] \) rather than \( x - [[h]] \).
This subtracts the vertical shift [[k]] instead of adding it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Taking the absolute value of the OUTPUT reflects any negative part of the graph up above the \( x \)-axis: \( g(x) = |f(x)| \). First \( f([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]])^{2} + ([[b]])([[x0]]) + ([[c]]) \), then \( g([[x0]]) = |f([[x0]])| = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the absolute value at all, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This negates the absolute value, which is never negative.
This applies the absolute value only to the leading coefficient instead of the whole output.
This applies the absolute value to the INPUT instead of the output, which is a different transformation.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Taking the absolute value of the INPUT replaces every \( x \) with \( |x| \) before applying \( f \): \( g(x) = f(|x|) \). So \( g([[x0]]) = [[a]]\,|[[x0]]|^{2} + ([[b]])\,|[[x0]]| + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to take the absolute value of the input at all, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This applies the absolute value to the OUTPUT instead of the input, which is a different transformation.
This applies the absolute value only inside the linear term, forgetting the squared term also uses \( |x| \).
This flips the sign of the linear term instead of substituting \( |x| \) consistently.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: First scale the input by [[d]], evaluate \( f \), then apply the outer vertical stretch [[c]]: \( g(x) = [[c]]\,f([[d]]x) \). So \( g([[x0]]) = [[c]]\Big([[a]]([[d]]\cdot[[x0]])^{2} + ([[m]])([[d]]\cdot[[x0]])\Big) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets both scale factors entirely, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This applies only the horizontal scale [[d]] and forgets the outer vertical stretch [[c]].
This applies only the outer vertical stretch [[c]] and forgets to scale the input by [[d]] first.
This scales the input correctly but forgets the outer multiplication by [[c]].
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). The graph of \( g \) is the reflection of the graph of \( f \) across the vertical line \( x = [[h]] \), so \( g(x) = f(2([[h]]) - x) \). What is \( g([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Reflecting across the vertical line \( x=[[h]] \) sends a point at distance \( d \) from \( [[h]] \) to the point at the same distance on the other side: \( g(x) = f(2([[h]]) - x) \). So \( g([[x0]]) = f(2([[h]]) - [[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to reflect at all, reporting \( f([[x0]]) \) itself.
This uses \( [[h]] - x \) instead of the correct \( 2\cdot[[h]] - x \) (missing the factor of 2 on the line's location).
This reflects about the \( y \)-axis ( \( x=0 \) ) instead of about the line \( x=[[h]] \).
This adds instead of subtracts \( x \) when reflecting about \( x=[[h]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: First shift the input right by [[h]] and evaluate \( f \), then reflect across the \( x \)-axis (negate), then shift up by [[k]]: \( g(x) = -f(x-([[h]])) + ([[k]]) \). So \( g([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the reflection (the negative sign) entirely.
This forgets the horizontal shift by [[h]], evaluating \( f \) at \( [[x0]] \) directly before reflecting.
This subtracts the vertical shift [[k]] instead of adding it.
This shifts left instead of right, using \( x + [[h]] \) rather than \( x - [[h]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x + ([[b]]) \) and \( g(x) = f(x - h) \) for some unknown shift \( h \). If \( g([[x0]]) = [[V]] \), what is \( h \)?
solution: Since \( g(x) = f(x-h) \), we have \( g([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]]-h) + ([[b]]) = [[V]] \). Solving for \( h \) gives \( h = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This negates the correct shift (a sign slip when isolating \( h \)).
This reports the input value \( [[x0]] \) itself instead of solving for the shift.
This subtracts in the wrong order, ignoring the coefficient \( [[a]] \) and constant \( [[b]] \) entirely.
This solves as if \( h \) were the input to \( f \) directly, ignoring the shift structure.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
